title: Yorkshire puddings
subtitle: Tall, hollow, and blistered from a screaming-hot tin
excerpt: Yorkshire puddings require a rested batter, a tin smoking hot with fat, and the oven door shut until they are fully risen. The rise is irreversible — opening the door before they are set will collapse them. A reliable recipe produces tall, airy puddings with crisp sides and a hollow centre.
sourceNotes: Yorkshire pudding originated as a way to make use of the dripping fat from a joint roasting above it on a spit. The batter was cooked in the fat drips, producing a dense savoury pancake. The modern version (tall and risen, cooked in a separate tin) emerged in the 20th century and is now the standard form. The principle has not changed: hot fat, cold batter, do not open the door.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze fully cooled puddings in a single layer, then bag. Reheat from frozen at 200°C for 5 minutes.
recipe.batchNotes: Make two or three batches at once — the batter is made in minutes and the puddings freeze well.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Rest the batter for at least 30 minutes, up to overnight in the fridge. The longer rest produces a better rise.

content.text: Equal volumes of eggs, flour, and milk is the reliable ratio: measure the eggs in a jug first, then match that volume in flour and in milk. Three large eggs is approximately 150 ml, which means 150 ml flour and 150 ml milk. This makes 12 standard puddings.

The fat in the tin must be smoking before the batter goes in. If it is merely warm, the batter will sit and steam rather than crisp. Use a tin with deep holes rather than shallow — the depth gives the pudding somewhere to rise. Do not open the oven for at least 20 minutes.

items.prepNote: or beef dripping, for the tin

content.text: Make the batter, blast the tin, bake

Whisk the {{eggs}} in a jug. Add the {{plain-flour}} and {{sea-salt-fine}} and whisk to a smooth, thick paste. Gradually add the {{whole-milk}}, whisking until fully combined and the consistency of thin cream. Rest for at least 30 minutes. Heat the oven to 220°C. Divide the {{vegetable-oil}} between the holes of a 12-hole Yorkshire pudding tin. Place the tin in the oven for 10 minutes, until the fat is smoking. Working quickly, pour the batter into the holes — fill each to half full. Return immediately to the oven. Bake for 20 to 25 minutes without opening the door, until deeply golden, risen, and crisp. Serve immediately.

items.symptom: Puddings rise then collapse in the oven
items.cause: Oven door opened too early, or oven temperature too low
items.fix: Do not open the oven before 20 minutes. The puddings must set before any cold air enters. If they collapse, they will not recover

items.symptom: Flat puddings that do not rise
items.cause: Fat not hot enough, or batter too thick
items.fix: The fat must be visibly smoking before the batter goes in. Check the consistency: batter should be thin cream, not thick pancake batter. Add a splash more milk if needed

items.cause: Insufficient fat, or tin not properly heated
items.fix: Use at least half a teaspoon of oil per hole. A well-seasoned tin or non-stick tin releases more reliably. If stuck, run a thin palette knife around the edge immediately out of the oven

content.text: Yorkshire puddings are eaten as part of a roast beef Sunday lunch throughout Britain, not just Yorkshire. They are served as a starter in some Yorkshire traditions — large individual puddings filled with gravy, eaten before the meat arrives. The individual portion has largely replaced the large flat version baked in a roasting tin, though both are correct and both follow the same method.
